clinical_history: This ECG, from a 69-year-old woman, is most consistent with which ONE of the following clinical scenarios?
diagnosis: The ECG shows marked sinus tachycardia (about 130 beats/min) at rest, consistent with neuroautonomic activation. A key clue is related to the large (notched in multiple leads and predominantly negative in V1) P waves. These P waves (previously referred to as P mitrale) are consistent with marked left atrial abnormality/enlargement. The only diagnosis listed that would account for the sinus tachycardia and left atrial overload is infective endocarditis/sepsis associated with a mitral valve bioprosthesis. The ECG also shows non-specific ST-T changes and relatively low limb lead voltage. Atrial septal defects are usually associated with right ventricular conduction delays. The S1Q3 pattern here with the sinus tachycardia is a non-specific finding but raises a question of pulmonary embolism (not present).
